acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] ErrorCode covers every entry in REALTIME_ERRORS.md and is the sole mapping to code strings.
- [ ] ErrorPayload serializes to both JSON (IPC) and CBOR (replication) with optional fields omitted when empty.
- [ ] Tests verify retryable and receipt fields are preserved and unknown codes do not break decoding.
description:
**Problem**
We need a single, truthful error vocabulary shared by IPC and replication. Today error mapping is scattered (OpError/CoreError/IpcError) and ErrorPayload in src/daemon/ipc.rs lacks retryable/receipt fields. That creates multiple representations of the same failure and forces clients to interpret strings ad hoc.

**Context**
- REALTIME_PLAN.md §0.14 (error payload standardization) and §9.10 (replication error handling)
- REALTIME_ERRORS.md (canonical error code registry)
- Current error plumbing: src/core/error.rs, src/error.rs, src/daemon/ipc.rs, src/daemon/repl/proto.rs (new)

**Files:** src/core/error.rs (new or expanded), src/error.rs, src/daemon/ipc.rs, src/api/mod.rs, src/daemon/repl/proto.rs, REALTIME_ERRORS.md
design:
**Design**
- Introduce ErrorCode enum with stable string mappings for every code in REALTIME_ERRORS.md; make it the single source of truth for code strings.
- Define ErrorPayload { code, message, retryable, retry_after_ms?, details?, receipt? } in core and re-use it in IPC JSON and replication CBOR.
- Provide From conversions from CoreError/OpError/IpcError/SyncError into ErrorPayload with consistent retryable/effect semantics.
- Enforce that unknown codes are tolerated by clients; only retry when retryable=true.
- Keep ErrorPayload schema version stable; add fields only as optional to preserve compatibility.
title: Phase 1: Core error types + ErrorPayload

description:
**Problem**
REALTIME_PLAN §3.1 requires rejecting non-canonical integer encodings in EventBody. Current decode uses minicbor::Decoder u64/u32 directly and never checks for non-minimal integer encodings, so overlong integer encodings are accepted.

Evidence:
- src/core/event.rs:1650–1667 decode_u32 uses dec.u64 with no canonicality check.
- No canonical integer validation exists in decode_map_len/decode_array_len/decode_u32.

**Why this violates plan**
Canonical CBOR is a correctness primitive for hashed payloads. Accepting non-canonical integers undermines deterministic validation requirements.

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Implement strict integer decoding that rejects non-minimal encodings (and tags).
- [ ] Apply to all integer fields in EventBody/TxnDelta/HlcMax.
- [ ] Add tests that reject overlong integer encodings.
title: CBOR decode does not reject non-canonical integer encodings

acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Unit test: encoding a tombstone with lineage=None produces correct map length.
- [ ] Unit test: decoding a tombstone with only one lineage field returns a validation error.
- [ ] Roundtrip test: tombstone with lineage Some(at,by) encodes and decodes identically.
- [ ] Regression test: validate_event_body rejects tombstone with partial lineage before encoding.
description:
**Problem**
encode_wire_tombstone can emit invalid CBOR if exactly one lineage field is set; debug_assert is skipped in release. This risks corrupt WAL/replication frames.

**Files**: src/core/event.rs, src/core/wire_bead.rs, src/git/checkpoint/*
design:
**Design**
Make tombstone lineage pairing a typestate invariant. Replace the two Option fields with a single Option<LineageStamp> (struct containing at+by). Encode only when lineage is Some. Add runtime validation that rejects partial lineage in decode or construction paths.

Also add a semantic validator hook for tombstones so invalid lineage can never reach encoding.

Update wire conversion helpers (checkpoint import/export) to use the new lineage representation.
title: Tombstone lineage pairing: make invalid lineage state unrepresentable

acceptance_criteria:
**Acceptance**
- [ ] Canonical set types exist in `beads-core` and enforce ordering/dedup at construction.
- [ ] All current normalizer helpers are removed or reduced to thin adapters to the new types.
- [ ] Checkpoint export, repl session, and mutation engine use canonical sets instead of raw vectors.
- [ ] Tests cover ordering/dedup stability and JSON roundtrip for at least two set types.
description:
**Problem**
Unordered collections are represented as raw `Vec`/`String` in multiple layers, and each call site re-sorts/dedups to get canonical order.

Examples:
- `normalize_namespaces` in `crates/beads-rs/src/git/checkpoint/export.rs`
- `normalize_namespaces` in `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/repl/session.rs`
- `canonical_deps` in `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/mutation_engine.rs`
- `DepSpec::parse_list` returns raw `Vec<DepSpec>` in `crates/beads-core/src/dep.rs`

This is scatter + drift: the "same" collection can have different ordering/dup semantics depending on which normalizer you remembered to call.

**Files:**
- `crates/beads-core/src/dep.rs`
- `crates/beads-rs/src/git/checkpoint/export.rs`
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/repl/session.rs`
- `crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/mutation_engine.rs`
- `crates/beads-core/src/collections.rs` (if adding set types)
design:
**Design**
Introduce canonical set types for frequently normalized collections and use them across boundaries.

Concrete plan:
1) Add canonical set wrappers in `beads-core`:
   - `NamespaceSet`
   - `DepSpecSet`
   - `LabelSet` (if not already modeled)
2) Construction performs sort + dedup and enforces any domain constraints.
3) Provide stable iteration order and `Serialize`/`Deserialize` as arrays.
4) Replace ad-hoc normalizers (`normalize_namespaces`, `canonical_deps`, etc.) with these types.
5) Update APIs to accept/return the canonical set types, not raw vectors.

**Design Notes**
- For performance, the set can store a `Vec` plus a flag that it is already canonical.
- Provide `from_vec_unchecked` only for trusted internal uses (tests/fixtures).
title: Canonical set types for unordered collections

description:
Problem: hotpath regressions are hard to catch without a standardized before/after compare in normal workflow.

Design:
- Add CI or pre-merge automation path that runs hotpath benchmark in controlled mode and reports deltas from baseline artifacts.
- Include thresholds for major regressions on ready/list/show/status hotpaths.

Acceptance:
- [ ] Automated path runs benchmark + compare
- [ ] Delta report is persisted as artifact
- [ ] Regression threshold policy documented
title: Add perf regression guardrail using hotpath benchmark artifacts

description:
**Problem**
beads-rs has hardcoded issue types (bug, feature, task, epic, chore). Users cannot define project-specific types (experiment, spike, incident, etc.). This limits expressiveness and forces users to shoehorn domain concepts into generic types.

**What Custom Types Enable (from Go beads)**
Custom types allow projects to define their own vocabulary:
- Research projects: experiment, hypothesis, analysis
- SRE: incident, postmortem, oncall
- Design: mockup, prototype, usability-test
- Any domain-specific concept

Configuration:
```toml
[types]
custom = ["experiment", "spike", "incident", "postmortem"]
```

Validation:
- Built-in types: Always valid (catch typos)
- Custom types: Valid per project (trust local config)
- Federation: Trust child repo's custom types (don't re-validate)

**Go Implementation**
Type validation (internal/types/types.go):
```go
// IsValid checks built-in types only
func (t IssueType) IsValid() bool {
    switch t { case TypeBug, TypeFeature, ... }
}

// IsValidWithCustom checks built-in + custom
func (t IssueType) IsValidWithCustom(customTypes []string) bool {
    if t.IsValid() { return true }
    for _, custom := range customTypes {
        if string(t) == custom { return true }
    }
    return false
}
```

Config integration (internal/configfile/config.go):
```go
type Config struct {
    Types struct {
        Custom []string `toml:"custom"`
    } `toml:"types"`
}
```

CLI usage:
- bd config set types.custom "exp,spike,incident"
- bd create --type experiment "Test hypothesis"
- bd list --type experiment

Federation trust model (lines 367-391):
When importing from child repo:
- Built-in types: Validate (catch typos like "tsak")
- Custom types: Trust (child already validated)
This implements "trust the chain below you" from HOP.

**Design for Rust**
Data model (src/core/):
```rust
pub enum IssueTypeVariant {
    Bug, Feature, Task, Epic, Chore,
    Gate, Agent, Role, Rig, Convoy, Event, Slot,
    Message, MergeRequest, Molecule,
    Custom(String),  // Project-specific types
}

impl IssueTypeVariant {
    pub fn is_builtin(&self) -> bool;
    pub fn validate(&self, custom_types: &[String]) -> bool;
}
```

Config (src/config/):
```toml
[types]
custom = ["experiment", "spike"]
```

Parse in Config struct:
```rust
pub struct Config {
    pub types: TypesConfig,
}

pub struct TypesConfig {
    pub custom: Vec<String>,
}
```

Validation:
```rust
impl Bead {
    pub fn validate(&self, config: &Config) -> Result<(), Error> {
        self.issue_type.validate(&config.types.custom)?;
        // ...
    }
}
```

CLI (src/cli/):
- bd config set types.custom "exp,spike"
- bd config get types.custom
- bd create --type <custom-type>
- bd list --type <custom-type>

**Design Notes**
- Custom types are strings, not validated at compile time
- Type aliases (feat → feature) still useful for custom types
- Federation: Import trusts source repo's custom types
- Consider: Type hierarchy/inheritance (spike extends experiment)
- Consider: Type-specific field requirements (validate by type)
- Consider: Type namespacing (rig:experiment vs hq:experiment)
- Consider: Type discovery (bd types list --all shows built-in + custom)

**Acceptance**
- [ ] IssueTypeVariant enum with Custom(String) variant
- [ ] Config.types.custom field
- [ ] bd config set/get types.custom works
- [ ] Validation allows built-in + custom types
- [ ] Validation rejects unknown types
- [ ] bd create --type <custom> works
- [ ] bd list --type <custom> filters correctly
- [ ] bd types command shows built-in + custom
- [ ] Federation import trusts custom types from source
- [ ] Tests for custom type validation
- [ ] Tests for config persistence
- [ ] Migration from Go beads preserves custom types in config

**Files to study in Go beads:**
- tmp/beads/internal/types/types.go (lines 518-529: IsValidWithCustom)
- tmp/beads/internal/types/types.go (lines 367-391: federation trust model)
- tmp/beads/internal/configfile/config.go (custom types config)
- tmp/beads/cmd/bd/create.go (type validation with custom)
title: Add custom types system support

acceptance_criteria:
Acceptance
- Update requests with title/description set to empty/whitespace are rejected with OpError::ValidationFailed.
- Types prevent constructing a patch with empty required fields after parsing.
- Tests cover update rejection and valid trimmed updates.
description:
Problem
- ParsedBeadPatch::parse only blocks Patch::Clear for title/description but allows Patch::Set("") or whitespace.
- Creates invalid state: required fields present but empty.
- Create path enforces non-empty; update does not.

Impact
- Invariant drift between create/update; data quality issues and UI regressions.
- Not encoded in types (string vs non-empty string).
design:
Design
- Introduce NonEmptyString (or reuse existing type) for required fields at the parse boundary.
- Normalize (trim) and reject empty values during ParsedBeadPatch::parse.
- Make CanonicalBeadPatch carry validated non-empty strings for title/description patches, so downstream code never sees invalid values.

Scatter fit
- Single validation point in parse; no downstream checks.

Files
- crates/beads-rs/src/daemon/mutation_engine.rs
- crates/beads-surface/src/ops.rs (if adding NonEmptyString)
- crates/beads-core/src/event.rs (if validating patch types)

title: mutation: required fields must be non-empty on update

description: Full cargo test failed: test_update_bead saw type task after update. Running the test alone and critical_path with --test-threads=1 passes. Likely shared runtime dir/daemon across parallel tests.
title: critical_path test_update_bead flaky in parallel runs

description:
Problem
The daemon trybuild guard `private_field_compile_fail_guards_acknowledgement_apis` takes 219s+ on CI cold runners and times out the fast or slow lanes depending on profile. PR #92 only widened the fast-profile timeout; the scheduled slow lane still kills the same test at the 120s profile ceiling. This contradicts docs/PERF_HOTPATHS.md receipts and makes main red for test-infra reasons.

Design
Treat this as a test-infra bug, not a timeout-tuning problem. Decide whether to (a) remove this trybuild guard from main CI entirely, (b) move it to a dedicated/manual/nightly lane, or (c) make its CI execution truthful by warming/caching the target/tests/trybuild artifact path or restructuring the test so it no longer does a cold compile of the full daemon dependency graph under nextest. Update nextest/workflow/docs together so CI policy matches the intended proof model.

Acceptance
- [ ] Main CI no longer exceeds budget because of this compile-fail guard
- [ ] The chosen policy for compile-fail checks is explicit in workflow/config/docs
- [ ] PERF_HOTPATHS receipts are refreshed or narrowed so they match the actual CI shape

Files: .github/workflows/ci.yml, .config/nextest.toml, crates/beads-daemon/tests/private_field_compile_fail.rs, docs/PERF_HOTPATHS.md
title: Compile-fail trybuild guard blows CI performance budget and times out in nextest
